summary: Fowler presents a humorous 'law' observing that software demonstrations fail more spectacularly when the stakes are highest. He notes this principle serves both as an observable truth and as a face-saving explanation after a demo disaster. The law applies to both the likelihood and severity of problems during important presentations.
key_points: Demo failures correlate positively with audience importance
The 'law' serves as a consolation when things go wrong
Both probability and severity of problems increase with stakes
money_quotes: The chances of a demo going wrong are directly proportional to the importance of the audience.
It also provides something of a sop when you explain it after everything has gone pear-shaped.
key_insight: High-stakes demos have a perverse tendency to fail precisely when success matters most, a phenomenon so universal it deserves recognition as a law.
